commentBody: Does close count?

"You're getting rid of all your old jewelry?"
"Yep! Playing ring toss."

("You didn't tell me about this."
"I didn't want to brooch the subject.")

Clever puzzle, but I didn't really love that there was a single rebus square that was symmetrically placed along the "ring" arc.  The "ring" squares all have a circle in them, which I assume represents the ring itself.  Maybe the "bottle" square could have been designated by something other than a circle, or maybe it could have been two vertical squares, or something.

I found this one to be a little chewy, taking me about 37% longer than my average Wed time. 

(Of course, I would allow for a bit of extra time because I was solving on my phone instead of my usual desktop.)

But early xwstats.com info agrees with me:

🌎  Global Stats
Difficulty
Very Hard
Median Solve Time
8:29
Median Solver
32% slower
⚡0% of users solved faster than their Wednesday average.
0% solved much faster (>20%) than their Wednesday average.
🐢100% of users solved slower than their Wednesday average.
70% solved much slower (>20%) than their Wednesday average.
First Wednesday fail probably ever. Singh clue completely stymied me.

That was a fun puzzle.  The ring toss theme was fun to discover, also I thought the cluing was fun in general.  Like GRAIL, GOSSIP, HOT DATE, good “Aha” style clothes.

Southwest corner was tough for me.  First I overthought the name of the game when the answer was simple.  But also BANA, ENGR, EVO.  I’m not familiar with two and ENGR is a nonsensical shortening, maybe the only bad clue of this puzzle.

Normally I hate single square rebusses especially on days that aren’t Thursday or Sunday but this one was obvious enough not to cause trouble.
Stormy little nor’easter in that top right corner but quite a fun puzzle. In my opinion, a great example of proper Wednesday challenge.
UPC for [Seller’s bottom lines, for short?] is brilliant. Fun Wednesday!
Using BREYER instead of DREYER for an "eponym" when Dreyer's actually uses an apostrophe to separate the s while Breyers does not seems like a decision made in bad faith.

Cute puzzle, and I liked the solo rebus square, although it took me a bit to figure it out. I had the I, N, and G in the circled squares and assumed that all five together spelled RINGS. Eventually I saw that S made no sense and that BOTTLE not only fit better, but made more sense of the RINGs.

I learned that RICIN was derived from castor beans from Breaking Bad, as a major plot line revolved around a plan to use Walter White’s homemade RICIN to kill drug lord Gus Fring. Although that plot never came to fruition the RICIN did play a part in series finale.  Chekov’s RICIN?

I got a laugh out of HOTDATE as the answer for [Organic chemistry experiment?].

I think this puzzle was dead solid perfect, especially for the day.

It gave me pause, sometimes rather lengthy pauses, but, though I had to work for it, it did eventually fit together. 

I loved RICIN because I loved "Breaking Bad" and loved to loathe Lydia.  Fans will know what I'm saying here.

And I liked PILOT for [Ace, e.g.].  I just can't hear the word "ace" with this meaning without seeing Snoopy sitting on his dog house, wearing goggles and a scarf around his neck.  "Curse you, Red Baron!"  Peanuts was huge when I was a kid.

But I have never heard of REXES.  I could google but I'm sure someone here will come to my aid.

On the harder side for a Wednesday. 

19A "Seller's bottom lines, for short?" is a terrible clue for UPC. UPC codes can be anywhere on product packaging, very often on the side of boxes and bottles.

I cannot remember a harder Wednesday. 
The SW just destroyed me. 

HOTDATE?
Carbon date. Age date.  
But I guess we’re talking about physical attraction. 

And I could not crack “eyeballed”. 

I don’t know what a BUGABOO is. But I definitely don’t equate to hearing word as a replacement for pet peeve. But that’s on me. 

I didn’t think my streak (not that I care about them) would die on a Wednesday. 
Wavelength?  My antenna was broken.
Thursday puzzle
Spoiler alert - why is the Gameplay up for Thursday already? I accidentally clicked on the wrong day. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/07/21/crosswords/daily-puzzle-2026-07-23.html

Ok. I am so very looking forward to reading the ensuing comments today.

For the record, I’m fine wjth this being a Wednesday, I loved it. But one has to wonder how in the world the editors thought this was the right day for it, based on the parade of Wednesdayish Thursdays lately. Are you seriously telling me it couldn’t have been switched with any number of recent Thursdays? I just don’t understand. I’ll be happy to switch my favorite day of the week to Wednesday if that’s what the editors really want.

That said, first of all, this was quite a chewy grid. Not surprising since the amount of theme material probably required some trickier entries (although overall I still thought this brilliantly constructed). But what really surprised me was a lot of trickier clueing for a Wednesday. I definitely had to stretch a bit to solve this.

I enjoyed the theme but I do think the full effect was not very intuitive. Thanks @BS for explaining it, it makes sense now. I wish the column had made it clear. I was imagining a top view looking at an array of of bottles, only one of which caught the ring, but that didn’t explain the arc or the RING letter in the other entries.

Anyway, I’m sure there will be a lot of complaints, but given the easier Thursdays lately turnabout is FAIR play. Thanks Rebecca for a great workout today. Your panache consistently brightens my crossword experience!
Glad toi see I am not alone. Much harder than your average Wednesday.
Ah, the dreaded unirebus. I completed the puzzle so in this case it didn't hamper me but, just on principle, I don't think it's fair to have only one rebus.

Yay me!  I got a rebus on my own!  

🥂 You are all invited to create a lovely cocktail (I think I'll have an aperol spritz) and  celebrate with me!

Beautiful and tough. Rebecca always ACEs constructions; I don't always ACE the solve. The challenging entries were wonderful. It was my own slowness that got in the way. I frequently stopped, pondered, had my memory jogged in other directions. SPIN THE BOTTLE reminded me, instead, of playing Twister with my friends, laughing so hard we had to take urgent bathroom breaks. STEP RIGHT UP brought to mind a song I haven't heard since the 70s when it played on the radio: https://youtu.be/nlpQdMdduX4?is=0Ucmd0FEEBQR9j63 LOX made me want to drop everything and run out and get a bagel with LOX and cream cheese stat! (I didn't; I had an English muffin with cream cheese instead. Didn't work; I still want the bagel.) And so it went. Not only wrangling with the clues, but going off on a tangent with the entries. And I loved it. Thank you, Rebecca. You're a crossword rock star!
A SEA would have got me SINGH and a NOTECARD a HOTDATE (who knew?) and a solve. I completely ignored the rebus and just went with BCAP. Carnival game was  a soft slow pitch.

My first nit to pick ever on a Goldstein and its more of the "I don't love the wallpaper" variety and, so, esthetic.

The course described by the  R - I - N - G on its way to the BOTTLE is erratic to the point of not physically consonant with how bodies in motion traverse arcs.

See?  Esthetic.

Otherwise fun and chewy Wednesday.

Oh I forgot to say...

When I thought 38D [Brief bathroom breaks, slangily] was singular, and I had STOP for the final letters and a P for the first letter...well, you can imagine some of the things I had in those spaces.  Even when I realized it had to be STOPS, I still had a corker.

I almost came to the comments to see if there was any freaking out.
Oof, the dreaded Whursday without warning.

The tricky clue for me was the ice cream.  There are both Dreyers and Breyers ice creams.  I couldn't come up with the wet habit.  I tried "spa".  Neither "adpt" or "abpt" were right, so I had to check the puzzle.  Otherwise, I enjoyed the puzzle.

For some reason the puzzle reminded me of this: https://www.allrecipes.com/recipe/9545/mock-apple-pie/ Ingredients that didn't seem to be adding up to a solve, but there it was—or at least almost. ABET popped into my mind when I read the clue, but I didn't really see how it would work until I came to the column. Congratulations Rebecca, on your clever cluing. Handful = STEIN? Got it. Now I want to go to the carnival.

Man, the techy biota of this puzzle -- OVO, LAN, ISP, UPC -- it's like the CIO roped in the chief ENGR to make a puzzle that would provide an outlet for those who work in places with a lot of outlets, i.e. Techies. It's not my world but I'm glad to have filled them all in dutifully, and I appreciate the Times for dragging us luddites forward. And yet there's spin the bottle, ring toss, and a hot date, all analog activities that don't require electricity (well, the last one does, rather). 

"Step right up," I love that phrase, maybe because of its appearance in Van Morrison's "Ballerina" off his masterpiece, Astral Weeks* -- a phrase filled with encouragement and doubt, welcome and challenge, recognition and dismissal. 

Loved seeing the summery APEROL, the menacing RICIN, and all the leonine Singhs out there, especially, for this admirer, Vijay Singh. Talk about aces! I still occasionally come across reference/video to his weird line-drive hole-in-one that skipped across the water at an Augusta Masters. Step right up, VJ!

The Pitt Stop near the University of Pittsburgh, still going strong since the 1990s at least, selling knockoff merch, or at least less costly Pitt apparel (alas, not aperol), souvenirs, plush toy Panthers and the like...Big, hearty Chas Bonasorte, chief glad-hander and owner, could often be found in front, in a muted carnival barker mode, talking about the old times and the new prospects. Step right up, Chas!
I’m over an hour in and not even halfway done. Slogging through a relatively unenjoyable puzzle. Feels more like a chore today.

I really liked this puzzle. :)

The rebus, the ringed letters, the clues, everything. 

The only thing I needed help with was "voluntold". Luckily my better half was familiar, so I then knew what to look for.

It took me longer than my Wed average, but that was a good thing since I got to enjoy it longer. :)
Is Breyers really an ice cream eponym? I would have said it is a brand name. And overall it was a fun puzzle.
TIL GOLDFISH and RINGTOSS have the same number of letters! And, both have “S” in the second-to-last spot.
This was very hard for a Wednesday, and the MIDI was a GenZ dream but a nightmare for this GenXer. I think it was my slowest MIDI ever!

41A [Surname that derives from the Sanskrit word for "lion"] I didn't know that the common name SINGH meant "lion." However, I am reminded of a movie called "Lion." I saw the film twice and listened to the autobiographical audiobook. The film stars Nicole Kidman and Dev Patel. It's great for this puzzle, because all you techies and MIT ENGRS will like its content. The film takes place in India and Australia. It received six Academy Award nominations including Best Picture. The name of the movie--"Lion"--is the English version of the main character's name. Very heartwarming story. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt3741834/

Thanks Rebecca for a nicely Toughened Up Wednesday. I needed to fill in a lot of the Downs before being certain enough to attempt the Acrosses. Trying Ham before LOX and Adze before RASP did not help me. Sotto Voce, as always, has an apt tune for the puzzle, but the first line of this one by the Tubes was rolling around in my head after getting 17 Across. https://youtu.be/aI2NE1VOZPw?si=To1defw3WhRklygJ
I’m only disappointed that there wasn’t animation at the end.
STEPRIGHTUP and HOTDATE in a carnival setting reminded me of this song (Caution: wildly inappropriate!) https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=mQ_k_VG6Syc

There was just so much that confused me in this puzzle! I was stumped for a long time by the theme, the unknowns the like of BUGABOOS and PIU, and I totally didn't vibe with the clueing. As a result, I found the grid the hardest in weeks.

I can never remember the name of American corkscrew pasta - over here shops and restaurants offer Polish and Italian style pastas, and corkscrews are called fusilli. It's the pasta I personally use most often, as sauces and cheese stick to it best. In fact, my "spaghetti" al ragu comes with fusilli.

Voluntold confused me in 12D - and apparently it confused Google, too: I just made a typo in the word at the beginning of this sentence, and autocorrect didn't notice or offer alternate spelling.

I was initially completely clueless regarding the theme. Finally I realized it would be about RING TOSS. I only know it from World of Warcraft, of all places. "Wesołe miasteczko" (literally, Happy little town) is the Polish equivalent of a carnival, and it features different games - however, I can't recall encountering ring toss there (but I last went 30 years ago or more).

I am a gamer but I play for my own personal fun, and thus I am not interested in competitions: I have no idea what EVO might be short for, and I relied exclusively on crosses to get the entry.

I almost completed the puzzle without aid, only to be defeated by the single rebus at the crossing of two unknowns 🤷🏽

Could somebody explain how BOTTLE relates to the circled RING?
Never heard of 'bugaboos' - Must be a US thing. Easy enough to infer from the other answers though, so no gripes here. All in all a nice, solid Wednesday puzzle! Well done!

Only slightly longer than my usual Wednesday. I caught onto the theme, but assumed it would be RINGS in the circles. When I got the "almost" screen, I had to flyspeck for a bit, and eventually realised that SPINTHES are not a thing. I didn't put in the rebus, and just assumed kids these days shorten everything, like sesh, totes and adorbs so SPIN THE B and B CAPS could be things that are said.

However, my favorite answer is BUGABOO. it reminds me of Frasier Crane. For those who haven't seen it, Frasier is one of the wittiest comedies of all time.

As I remember, we are talking around sixty-five years ago, the ring toss with bottles involved small brown rings and the bottles were the old Coke bottles in their wood cases.  Another toss game was wooden hoops onto large wooden pegs (for want of a better word). Either way, a lot of quarters changed hands with guys trying to win stuffed animals for their girls. I said it was a long time ago.
I am bracing for disgruntlement among the cruciverbal commentariat.
my first try for 14a was COPAY,  that would have been clever
Not a bad puzzle and not too hard for a Wednesday but it always feels a little wrong having rebus answers outside of Thursday  (and very occasionally Sunday)

Tough, tough tough …
1 down: __ ss __    Had to be diss related?
Only after sleeping on it, clearing the entire NW corner did GRAIL hove into view and GOSSIP was a proper bolt from the blue.

My sister had a REX when she lived in Devon (but I think it was Cornish); I’m an ENGR and have seen all manner of abbreviations on org charts; RICIN was this can’t be true … but the interweb confirmed it; an Italian SPRITZ in Milano (great colleagues there).

And then onto the fly specking: 43across AdET makes no sense - TIL there is an ice-cream company in the US called BREYERS and also one called dREYERS.

Meh, meh, meh … when filling in the puzzle, I had figured youngsters spin all manners of things now, so SPINTHEs made sort of sense. I also assumed that an s-CAP was a particular type of bottle top. When the rebus suddenly came to me, I’m sure the bright light that flashed on over my head woke the cat.

Tough, tough, tough but fair. And fun. Would repeat the experience if I could

Lovely puzzle. Enjoyed the rebus on an unusual day! Finding one on a Thursday feels like just doing my job, finding one, unlooked for, on Wednesday is a little more satisfying. 

Otherwise, never run across “voluntold” before. Funny word, I guess it does kinda make sense

What a nice Wednesday treat! Quite a bit of "not the first thing I thought of" answers, which makes it typical Rebecca Goldstein. The single rebus, with the ring moving onto the bottle was a little extra flourish which, for me, put it into early week POY consideration. STEP RIGHT UP: https://youtu.be/kTdScE3Rqh8?si=8ClZOVIvL_xRxefN

I got stalled because my original answer for 19A: "Seller's bottom lines, for short?" was OBO (Or Best Offer).  I was totally certain that could be the correct answer. I solved it through the crossings and by employing one of my cardinal solving rules: "Maybe I was wrong."

Slower than my Wednesday average, which seems typical today.

Baskin before BREYER, mdS before DRS, bible before CANON, adze before RASP. Plus a few I just didn't know: REXES, PIU, EVO.

Definitely enough of a challenge to make me glad I got it at all.

Thanks!

A spot-on visual representation of a RING TOSS, a delightful supporting cast of theme answers, a solo rebus, the sing-song BUGABOOS, plus cluing vagueness to slow down the uncovering and give the brain some work to do – joy in the box for me today.

Even more joy from a TIL – “voluntold”, clever clues for UPC and HOT DATE, and the splendidly silly clue for SEA: [Habitat with a wet floor].

And, just a bit of evidence of Rebecca’s skill as a constructor… Up in the NE corner, five letters of theme answers PLUSH TOY and SPIN THE BOTTLE abut, and look at how cleanly she filled that corner.  Brava!

YUM all around for what you created here, Rebecca.  Thank you so much!

Pretty hard for a Wednesday, but fun nevertheless.  

On a side note, Sam probably wasn't the only one that put sorry for 14A (definitely guilty here).

Such a great summer puz.
UPC got me REALLY missing price stickers. I recently moved, and I'm my new grocery store, to see the price of an item on the bottom shelf, instead of just picking the item up, you literally have to get on hands and knees to see the shelf sticker which is 2 inches from the floor! Fine then give me some kneepads with my shopping cart!
Somehow tech always gets just slightly ahead of practicality so as to make life MORE challenging than it was before the tech. Slightly, that is, until ai (the hippie dippie in me refuses to capitalize). I fear we'll be at least figuratively on hands and knees before long unless someone(s) reigns it in a bit (a lot).... 
Hopeful thought for the day!

Thanks for a great Wednesday puzzle. Nice to bring up carnival memories from my childhood and good times with my children. Step right up and try your luck made for great memory evokers!. And a straight forward rebus to boot.😊
This was great. More Wednesdays at this level please. And rebuses every day of the week!

In 1914, in the *Zeitschrift für Ethnologie," musicologists Erich von Hornbostel and Curt Sachs a system, modeled after the Dewey Decimal System in which, with typical German rigor, they attempted to categorize all musical instruments.
In the Hornbostel-Sachs system, all string instruments, or chordophones, have the top-tier designation of "3." Harps and Lyres both fall into the second-tier category 32--"complex chordophones", as opposed to 31 "simple chordophones," such as zithers or pianos. ("Simple" being a relative term.)

But there the family tree bifurcates: Harps have the third-tier designation 322--"Complex chordophones in which the strings lie perpendicular to the sound-box," as opposed to 321 " . . . in which the strings lie parallel to the sound-box," and are generically termed "lutes," but include guitars, violins, sitars, theorbi, and, yes, lyres. 

Lyres are 321.2--" . . . in which the strings are attached to a yoke which lies in the same plane as the sound-table and consists of two arms and a cross-bar." They are further divided into 321.21, "bowl lyres," and 321.22 "box lyres" based on the shape of the resonator.

And now you know.
Nice challenge! Clever with a perfect Wednesday level of stickiness.

Nice enjoyable Wednesday puzzle.

The rebus was pretty straightforward for me, as the clue "Risqué game that teenagers might give a whirl?" seemed obvious to anyone who has ever heard of the game.

But the puzzle was surprisingly tricky, with its many misdirects (I'm looking at you, "Organic chemistry experiment?"), and most surprising was that my trouble areas were the Northeast and Southwest, where the many three-letter answers normally indicate ease.

Thanks, Rebecca Goldstein, for a nice Wednesday workout.

Interesting entries and clues. Theme seemed weak, hence the circles and a rebus square in an attempt to "strengthen" it. Still seemed weak to me.

Nice argument in favor of having an occasional themeless Wednesday puzzle.

After two days of trying to solve using just the horizontal clues, I approached this one in the usual way. This made it seem remarkably easy ... but in reality it still took me 14 minutes.

Fine Wednesday puzzle. But the midi was impossible for me. And the wordplay column was already posted for tomorrow but dated July 21. I live alone and was afraid I had lost my mind..and a day... for a bit.
Terrific puzzle but certainly not a gimme. It had me fighting for every answer.
EVO seemed obscure to me.

Enjoyable puzzle overall, breezy with some chewy moments. I am in agreement with others who found the theme a bit weak. Having actual RINGs in the other circled squares would have raised the bar.

But can I really be the first to object to the clue "Some STEM degrees"? The Ph.D. is not particularly a STEM degree (the clue is in the name, as they say). Would have been better clued as "some are STEM degrees" vel sim.

This one was tough for me…finished about 40% above my average time…but, honestly, I’m satisfied as I was able to finish with no help. At first, when I solved 23D/52A, I thought,  “That’s odd…only one rebus.” When I finished, and looked at the finished grid, however, I thought, “Aha! Perfect.”  Thanks to Rebecca Goldstein for a good start to my day.

Weeeeellllll...  
SPIN THE BOTTLE is "Risque"??????????????????
What is wrong with y'all?  
Or (worse):  what constitutes that game in CrossWorld???

I have all but one of the squares filled correctly on my page, and I stared at SPIN THE ( ) because crossing it was "( ) CAP" for "Pop top?"
I took POP to mean Old Guy/Dad/DHubby--lovers of CAPs-- so I spent some time wondering, "Do old guys call them " S CAPs?..."

And there was all the (apparently superfluous) carny-game stuff like RING TOSS and  a RING+one more circle, and a PLUSH TOY  and the Barker's spiel, none of it actually relating to SPIN THE (mystery item/S).  Even with the look-up (BOTTLE) it doesn't fit a carnival/street fair, because that is a party game--which is pretty tame-- and it doesn't fit in here.

(WE do not call a cola A  SOFT DRINK or A POP  around here...)

I need to to lie down.

I thought this was a pretty nifty puzzle and not as inconsistent with Wednesday standards as some have opined. It was a little mucky in the west central area of the puzzle, giving it a dash of Thursday flavor. I'll take a puzzle with both aperol and bugaboos in it.
Pretty campy theme imho.
I didn't enjoy this one much.  Some odd clues.
Sir, there’s a rebus in my Wednesday!

The difference between a carnival ring-toss game and this somewhat-wonky puzzle is that I come here each day having paid for and even hoping for a bit of cheatery and off-balance slipperiness. Today’s square dance managed to TIEUP my ATTN for a while. The wordplay is plushy enough for me. Fun.

(Could PITSTOPS  be [Shoulders]?)

.

Agreeing with the masses that this was very hard for a Wednesday.  Miraculously, I made no errors.  ROPEDIN was easy because of all the crossing letters.  Other than that, I was flummoxed about it until googling VOLUNTOLD.  Cute word.  Never never heard of aperol, but SIR had to be right so I got that one as well.  RANOVER gave me EVO, which I still don't understand, but I don't do video game competitions.  Heck, I don't even do video games.  And while I'm a pet afficionado, and love cats almost as much as I love dogs, I've never heard of REXES.  And just one more letter to talk about.  The I of CIO.  I figured TAICHI was a better way to spell than TAECHI, so I got that letter as well, even though I don't understand how the Congress of Industrial Organizations got into this puzzle.  Thank you, Rebecca, for a thoroughly enjoyable workout.  Seriously.  It was fun.  Hard puzzles are fun.

Thanks, Rebecca, I quite enjoyed this puzzle.
And yay for a rebus. It was a very easy one but enough to add a little spice to a Wednesday.

Wow, that was one crunchy Wednesday. I really struggled to get a toe hold until I reached MOSHE. For some arcane reason his name and image has always stuck in my head.

I like the funfair theme. It took a while, but I got the hang of it in the end, including that mischievous rebus.

We have friends coming to stay this evening. We’re very partial to an APEROL spritz thank you very much. The Prosecco is already cooling in the fridge.

Now, REXES. The resident Devonian aka husband, had never heard of nor seen the breed, despite being born a whisker away from Buckfastleigh. We had to google them. I put a shout out to the extended family, split between Devon and Cornwall, but nobody else had come across them either. Odd. I’m not particularly a feline aficionado, much more of a dog person, though the resident Shiba is more cat than dog in his habits. But, without wishing to offend any owners out there, they’re a bit odd looking, no?

On today's midi. I don't see how the word "snap" has any connection whatsoever to "show praise." Huh? Plus the trivia was extremely obscure. C'mon! I assume the midi is for mere mortals like me who haven't yet been initiated into the NYT crossword cult!
Nae to the naysayers

When I saw Rebecca’s name, I knew I was in for a good time. 

I expected to see more grumbling about the (single!) Wednesday rebus, and am happy to see most people were fine with it. I like a puzzle that keeps me on my toes!

One slight peeve: I’ve never heard of BUGABOO equating to “pet peeve”. The dictionary defines it as an object of fear or worry, possibly derived from “bogeyman”. A pet peeve doesn’t frighten, it just annoys.

2D sent me straight to “fusilli”, since Seinfeld taught me that that is the most corkscrew-y of pastas. (“A million to one shot, Jerry! A million to one!”) But that didn’t fit, so ROTINI it was.

Best Wednesday in recent memory. One of the best Thursdays, too.
ENGR for engineer?? 🤨
EXCELLENT! Loved the theme, construction, cluing, the whole darn thing. I ran a couple minutes over the average time and loved every second. Thank you!
Really terrible single rebus.
Really enjoyed how free-wheeling this felt. The theme's visuals fitting into the puzzle and then that single rebus square. It all clicked for me, leading to a fast and fun solve!

I think maybe long-time commenters/solvers have been unintentionally doing newer solvers a disservice by emphasizing that rebus puzzles can appear on Thursdays and Sundays. They can also appear on other days. The day with the most rebus puzzles after those two days is Wednesday.  There have been 39 Wednesday puzzles with a rebus feature since 2010. Most of these have had a single rebus, but not all.  The records at xwordinfo.com go further back than 2010, but that's when I quit counting.
Really fun! My only complaint is that my rebus auto filled on its own when I typed "B" before I could do anything else. Was that supposed to happen??
My gut tells me there's not a ton of overlap among the FGC (fighting game community) and those doing the NYT crossword so EVO probably made no sense to most, but was a welcome surprise for me 🤷‍♂️
Most Excellent Wednesday! Hoping it bodes well for the end-of-week offerings…

I was really groovin and cruising until I was forced to contend with the SW corner. Lost count how many times I: switched between mdS and DRS, gaslit myself into thinking BANA wasn't in Troy, wondered if a fox was considered a feline in other countries, and debated if it was called a coin TOSS or RING. Oof!!!

I absolutely despised this slop. Bad construction, bad editing (should have been rejected). 

First, however, I LOVED the theme!! 

I have no problem with a rebus puzzle being used on any day, I do have a problem with it being used once in a puzzle. That is called slop. That is when the constructor could not complete the puzzle so they add one rebus. 🤦🏻‍♂️ 

Add to that many obscure trivia entries and you have a miserable puzzle.

So much promise….

Rating:
2/10
Wholly unenjoyable and with a worthless rebus to show the constructor’s failure at constructing? Hard pass.
Too artificially hard for Wednesday and a useless rebus? Just no.

Blech. I hate single rebus puzzles. Always feels like weak construction. And that SW is just a total mess. I would bet my house that the constructor finished there and just had to google anything that would fit
I just got absolutely stumped by the NE corner for some reason. Seemed much more difficult than the average Wed.

Puzzle find today - a Sunday from June 19, 1966 by Anne Fox with the title: "Paterfamilias." Some Answers in that one: MADEAFAUXPAS MYFATHER ANDYVARIPAPA POPGOESTHEWEASEL MYHEARTBELONGSTODADDY POPTUNES POPOVERS And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=6/19/1966&h=53a ....

I confidently started off 1A with WALDO.

I guess GRAIL works too.

Good fun puzzle with some surprises and a bit over average time to complete ( fir me, obv.).

Delight Checklist: what the constructor or editors are hoping vs. .... what I experienced:
1. Rebus! I'll bet they love that!! .... I don't. I like crossword puzzles, not treasure hunts. 
2. Ha! I'll bet it'll confuse them that there is only one rebus! ... it did confuse me, but not in a good way. Either all the circles should be rebi, or none. 
3. Wacky Wednesdays are a great way to throw off the staid expectations!!.... No, just no. Thursdays are for the rebi, Wednesdays are not. If this is to change, someone make an announcement. 
4. All will be forgiven when they see that "ring" is spelled out..... No, all is not. It's not that hard to find letters to circle and then spell something. But in this case, it's unclear if the constructor has ever seen a ring toss. There are MANY bottles, not one, all set in a grid. A ring is a thing thrown, not a region on the playing surface. 
5. Even if they don't like every aspect, they'll be happy with the creativity and fun!!! Nah. It was aight but there must have been more coherent puzzles to publish. 
P.S. So, from time to time I critique a puzzle. This I do because I like discussion of the subject and like bringing matters to the editors' attention. Without exception when I do post such a letter, I'm informed that I am a curmudgeon with no love of sunlight and rainbows who should, presumably, fall in line with everyone else who loved the puzzle. I pre-reject such attacks on my character. Can't we just talk puzzle and not personality?

Well, that was a fun game, but I prefer trying to shoot out the star with the full-auto BB gun, And now I'm craving a funnel cake. Thumbs up for a surprise rebus on a Wednesday, even if it was just a singleton.
i hated this one
What a mess, hints that make no sense, no rhyme or rhythm. This wasn't clever or hard, just plain weak. Made me rethink my subscription.

I really disliked this puzzle. As a casual solver, I typically get Wednesdays in full about 75% of the time and am a clue or two away the rest of the time. Not today. Way too difficult for a Wednesday. As casual solvers, we only get three or four puzzles a week; it stinks to have one taken away. Oh well.
For once I was on the faster side of the grain for a Very Hard rated puzzle. Sometimes you just jive with a puzzle and this was it. Except ENGR of course :)
What a fun puzzle!
Enjoyed this one. Ring toss games always seemed fixed to me so never would pay to play.

I was busy today and almost skipped doing the puzzle. But once I saw that Rebecca Goldstein is the constructor, I made time for it! She’s one of my favorite constructors, always a fun solve. Nice one!
Oh boy. A gimmicky rebus puzzle. How cute. They're the only puzzles I actually feel bad about solving. Maybe tomorrow a good, well constructed crossword?
Perfect Wednesday puzzle!! I'm always happy on the rare days when there are no clues about basketball.
Sam, didn't the Canadian in you want to put sorry, eh? Asking for a friend,
It was a fine puzzle except for the trespassing rebus.  Let the rebus find its own puzzle.....in its own category and not with the crossword.  Like meat and dairy, lets keep them separate.

Apparently, a lot of people found this tough for a Wednesday. I didn't find it tough, per se, but I thought that it had an irritating number of [abbr.]s and [for shorts]s. I'm a sucker for single-rebus puzzles, however. 

I was a little disappointed, though, that 60A wasn't a convention-breaking repeat of STEP RIGHT UP.

Re: Apocrypha
I initially wrote Bible (Episcopalian, obviously). When it did not work out, I ended up at canon but thought it worth raising what I found in following up. The Apocrypha are a point of division within the various sects of Christianity and are canon to some Christians (Roman Catholics and some orthodox sects) but not canon others (Episcopalians and Protestants). 
I do not think NYT intended to take a stance on this schism but it's funny to think about how something like this made it past editors.
i'm new to this and don't understand how to complete the puzzle. how do i enter letters for a rebus square?
You get lox at an appetizing store, NOT a deli.

Loved this puzzle!  Question for the group:  I generally can solve without any lookups.  I usually have a consecutively solved streak of 20, 30, or 40 days before I have to look up an answer or turn on auto-check.  In the last two weeks my solve rate has plummeted to about 60%. I had surgery two weeks ago and have been on pain meds.  So here’s my question:  have the last two weeks been noticeably more challenging for anyone else? Or should I blame my results on the meds?  If you read this far, thanks for indulging me.

Wow. A lot of people have strong feelings about today’s  puzzle. I guess a rebus will bring out the worst in some. I, for one, enjoyed this crunchy Wednesday! Difficult but not impossible, and only two lookups for me (a name and a term I was unfamiliar with). Thanks for the brain workout!
Very enjoyable. Took me an hour-plus but I got through it without any cheating. It was a Thursday so I guess the constructor felt obliged to sneak in a rebus.

Haven't read the nearly 300 pieces of chitchat you all have provided for me but, even in my current ignorance, I'm happy to say, Welcome back, Rebecca! Delighted to see you again.
Now, off to review the nitting!
I thought 41 across was BENGE, and therefore,39 down PEESTOP.🙊🙊

I've always thought that a puzzle with one rebus is a delicious kind of devious. 

At least I only filled in one rebus. There may be others that I missed. 

It makes me control my "OMG, I'll have to go back and re-check everything" impulse and just let things be as they were solved.

The fewer three letter answers the better.  Fourteen is a few more than that when paired with a whole lot of fours.  (Eighteen, I think.  I got dizzy trying to count them.)

I'd rather like a puzzle with fourteen three letter eighteen four letter clues.

poem made from words in today’s puzzle


a/ in search of a lion 
i see you and i’m thinking
of life in its dark and wild forms
you are the lion
derived from the temperature and chemistry of wood and alum
d/ you play a game
with the body 
parts of chipmunks 
you 
a/ step true
Late to the PARTY , but I want to SING(H)! This was a wonderful Wednesday.

I liked this challenging puzzle - made me think and puzzle over the clues.  But I was stumped by a Wednesday rebus and did not put together the now obvious "bottle" that the rings are tossed at.  Still very satisfying.

You'd think it would just sink in to the rebus-haters at some point that rebuses (rebi?) have been around forever, many people love them, and they aren't going away. But no, even when there's a single, pretty-easy-to-figure-out rebus, the usual chorus of complaints arises. Let it go already!

Before our careers in heating and air conditioning repair took off, King Arthur and I were both carnival barkers.  They called us the “Kings of the Midway,” even though Art was technically the only royalty among us.  We had all the games: dunk tank, water gun race, balloon darts, guess your weight, strength tester, and of course, the crown jewel of the midway, the RINGTOSS. I’ll never forget the time Percival rolled through and hit three for three right on the cup. A perfect round. And if that wasn’t enough, it turned out “the cup” was actually the Holy GRAIL.  As I live and breathe, my brothers. This thing was just sitting there the whole time, right under our noses!  Well, you can bet your cadusia we never let Arty live that one down.

For online solvers, this would have been a great opportunity for some animation along with the happy music.

Still, a very nice Wednesday.
I got hung up becauise Dreyer is ALSO an ice cream eponym.
Really liked the puzzle and the rebus but the SW corner defeated me—I couldn’t complain of a natick, but EVO, engr, and others defeated me.
Too hard for a Wednesday – which would not be a problem if at least I’d had some fun.
I cam for the comments and wasn't disappointed.  Cool puzzle with a neat twist...off cap.
I doubt very much that Spin the Bottle is played by today’s teenagers. I am 71, and even way back then we played it in middle school. Unless the “prize” is much more than a kiss.

l like the portmanteau VOLUNTOLD.

Rebus- not a fan. Sorry.

All good and dandy, but putting a person who was in a high position in the IDF while Israel stole and harassed and plundered and overtook Palestine in the middle of the last century certainly seems like a bad choice. I will probably not be continuing my subscription due to this.
What a fun and clever puzzle! Delightful!

@Francis Devon Rexes are a breed of cat. I have two - littermate sisters! They are often known as the "monkey" of the cat world. They have curly hair and big ears, but its their personality is a lot of fun. They are acrobatic and smart, and like to perch on shoulders and really be involved.

Yes, on the harder side for a Wednesday. If the all the reccos are for that statement, fine; if they are for the comment on 19A, boo! First rule of crossword clues and answers is that any case makes them valid, it need not be every case. The cereal box I bought a few days ago has the UPC on the bottom. The clue is terrific wordplay.

@Dave K. Terrible?? Geez
Yes some clues are better than others, but when I learned that clues aren't definitions it opened my mind more to clever clueing. And when there's a ? in the clue all bets are off!
If you really wanted to suss out the clue, why is it line and not lineS?
For this one, maybe UPC could also - more literally - mean the price, or bottom line?
The clue uses lines, not line. A UPC is lines.

userLocation: Under the rainbow. It's the smoke.
commentBody:
@Dave K.
The bottom line of every UPC bar code is the numerical code. If you can't scan it, you can type in the code. Made perfect sense to me! But then, I'm rarely sensible.

@Barry Ancona oopsy
Then the clue's even betters than I thought!

@Barry Ancona
Between bottom lines, betting lines, and geometric lines,
+ stock as in stock market and stock as in a store's contents
+ market as in store and market as in stocks and bonds 
+ short as in abbreviated and short as in selling short
there are dozens of variations to clue this.

@Weak

It was tricky in places.  I didn't really expect HOT DATE for the Organic chemistry experiment.  And, while I appreciated the misdirection, I was hoping for something genuinely O-chem related.  Your suggestion of "carbon date" would have been great, but maybe a bit controversial.

This puzzle contained an animal I've never heard of, a liqueur I've never heard of (even at the Minnesota School of Bartending, of which I am a proud alum), a liqueur drink I've never heard of (but was pretty easy to guess), and a surname I've come across at times, but never knew was a lion.

So it was no gimme.

@Weak

This is not a bitter comment because I couldn’t solve. But honestly, I wasn’t too impressed with RING being part of an entry and the circled letters. 

I know there are no ‘rules’ per se.  But I thought inelegant.  I actually questioned my RINGTOSS entry because of the circles.  

Sorry. I’m sure many of you thought this was brilliant. And I know this sounds like sour grapes. But my opinion, regardless of the color of my star.

@Weak

Everyone is allowed to not like some puzzles.
@Weak The puzzle has been out for less than 2 hours. Why not step away / sleep on it? Seemed like typical Wednesday difficulty to me. I’ll grant that RASP / TIA / PIU was a hard crossing.

@Bennett

Fair question. I guess because it’s a Wednesday and I only had some cells in the southwest that I couldn’t crack. I didn’t care enough  try and sleep on it. If I had blanks all over the puzzle, I probably would have put it down and returned.  

Also, I don’t care about streaks so a blue star is not a huge deal for me. That said I probably would have put the puzzle down if this was a Saturday.

Everyone has their own strategies and reasons for doing the crossword. I am thankful that it is there for me on a daily basis.

@Matt

Not parabolic enough for you, huh?

@Matt

You must have really good eyes.    ;-)

If we assign coordinates to the centers of the five circles, say with the origin being at the center of the leftmost circle, then our five points are (0,0), (1,2), (4,4), (7,2), (8,0).

Of course, these five points can be fit perfectly by a quartic polynomial. But, indeed, you are correct that no quadratic polynomial does this. 

It seems that the polynomial 

-0.252874 x^2+2.02299 x+0.0827586

comes closest (in the least-squares sense). 

It's not exact, but the R-squared coefficient of determination of the fit seems to be 0.993432, with 1 being a perfect fit.

(And, of course, the ring isn't tossed in a vacuum, so the true arc won't be perfectly parabolic in any case.)
I hope Deb Amlen isn't reading this thread...

I'm more concerned for Andrzej.

@Xword Junkie
Well, if you want the parabola to pass through the *center* of each circle, sure. Personally, I'd give each coordinate a tolerance of +/- 0.5. 

OK, +/- 0.4, given that the circle borders have finite thickness.

Next question: how many decimal places can you trim off that way?

Of course I agree with you. I just had to give you a hard time.

I had a prof who said he took a government test once that asked about the shape of the trajectory of a projectile. The possible answers were:
a) part of a circle
b) part of a parabola
c) part of an ellipse
d) part of a spiral

Instead of trying to figure out the best answer, he sat there evaluating the probable level of education of the test constructor.

@Andrzej 
Ring toss is a classic carnival game where the player throws plastic rings into a grid of glass bottles in an attempt to get the ring string the neck of any bottle. The bottles are usually an uncomfortable distance away and the ribs bounce an extraordinarily maddening amount off the top of the glass.

I just saw the post of @BS below. So you toss the rings onto bottles! In WoW it was just pegs...

So yeah, I was destined to fail this puzzle - the cultural differences defeated me, as they are bound to from time to time.

@David

Thanks. The WoW version I was familiar with had my character toss rings onto a row of five pegs. I had no idea a grid of bottles came into it in real American life.

You can be the first to object because others understand that crossword answers need not be every example of the clue (and vice versa); any example will do. Normal crossword clue.

@CAR the clue is not incorrect (as the regulars will be happy to point out), It’s just an example of bad clueing - especially for a Wednesday. You are right to point it out.

The inclusion of “STEM” in the clue is to indicate an abbreviation or initialism, which is actually unnecessary in this case since PhD is already a much more common way to refer to the degree than “Doctor of Philosophy.”

@Asher B.

I’ll talk puzzle and certainly not attack your character, I thought yours was a very thoughtful, coherent critique, even the parts that I don’t agree with you as much about, and I don’t think you are a curmudgeon.

I tend to agree with you about the design. I too was looking at it from the top and expecting many bottles and didn’t understand the other rings. As it is I’ll accept the premise of the sideways view, with one ring  essentially arcing up and finally landing around the bottle—and I enjoyed the conceit—but would certainly agree it just doesn’t quite work. I just let it slide because I enjoyed the rest of it quite a bit (your point #5).

As for the rest, I personally don’t mind a rebus, single or not, on a Wednesday if it’s a bit more obvious, nor challenging fill, etc. But what I certainly don’t get is, if they are trying to sort out Wednesdays vs Thursdays, why are they giving us much more boring Thursdays and then throw this into a Wednesday? If we were getting really chewy Thursdays that this was one step down from, I’d understand—but we are not.
@SP Thank you for the relevant and intelligent points. I am not clear what the Times is doing with the Wednesday/Thursday thing. And I agree that the whole puzzle just doesn't quite work.

@Asher B.

I will try to talk purely puzzles without dissing you as a person.  (BTW, most here only do that when we feel some unfair criticism or insult has been dealt them by the commenter.)

I'm not much comfort, I'm afraid.  I like many here are in my twilight years, and I can feel my mental sluggishness accumulating, I think.

The best I've been able to do is to watch it happen, out of curiosity.
